grammar_points.title: Subjunctive vs Indicative Contrasts
grammar_points.short_description: When the same conjunction triggers different moods
grammar_points.explanation:
Some conjunctions take either the **indicative** or **subjunctive** depending on meaning.

**aunque (although/even if):**
- + indicative (fact): Aunque llueve, salgo. (Although it IS raining, I go out.)
- + subjunctive (hypothetical): Aunque llueva, saldré. (Even if it rains, I will go out.)

**cuando (when):**
- + indicative (past/habitual): Cuando llego, como. (When I arrive, I eat.)
- + subjunctive (future): Cuando llegue, comeré. (When I arrive [future], I'll eat.)

**donde (where):**
- + indicative (known): Voy donde vivo. (I go where I live.)
- + subjunctive (unknown): Iré donde tú digas. (I'll go wherever you say.)

**como (as/how):**
- + indicative (fact): Como dije, es importante. (As I said, it's important.)
- + subjunctive (warning): Como no estudies, suspenderás. (If you don't study, you'll fail.)

**Key principle:** Indicative = certain/known. Subjunctive = uncertain/hypothetical.

examples.sentence: Aunque llueve, vamos al parque.
examples.translation: Although it's raining, we're going to the park. (fact)

examples.sentence: Aunque llueva mañana, iremos al parque.
examples.translation: Even if it rains tomorrow, we'll go to the park. (hypothetical)

grammar_points.title: Third Conditional
grammar_points.short_description: Impossible past conditions and their consequences
grammar_points.explanation:
The **third conditional** talks about impossible past conditions — things that didn't happen.

**Formation:**
- Si + pluperfect subjunctive, + conditional perfect
- Si hubiera estudiado, habría aprobado. (If I had studied, I would have passed.)

**Examples:**
- Si hubiera sabido, te habría llamado. (If I had known, I would have called you.)
- Si no hubiera llovido, habríamos ido a la playa. (If it hadn't rained, we would have gone to the beach.)
- Si hubieras venido, te habrías divertido. (If you had come, you would have had fun.)

**Alternative with pluperfect subjunctive in both clauses:**
- Si hubiera estudiado, hubiera aprobado. (same meaning, more colloquial)

**Expressing regret:**
- Ojalá hubiera estudiado más. (I wish I had studied more.)
- Debería haber ido. (I should have gone.)

examples.translation: If you hadn't said that, we wouldn't be here.

grammar_points.title: Emphatic Structures
grammar_points.short_description: Ways to add emphasis and focus in Spanish
grammar_points.explanation:
Spanish uses several **emphatic structures** to highlight information.

**Lo que... es (que)...** (What... is that...):
- Lo que me molesta es que no escucha. (What bothers me is that he doesn't listen.)
- Lo que necesitamos es más tiempo. (What we need is more time.)

**Es... quien/donde/cuando...** (It is... who/where/when...):
- Es Juan quien lo hizo. (It is Juan who did it.)
- Fue aquí donde la vi. (It was here where I saw her.)

**Sí que + verb** (emphatic affirmation):
- Sí que me gusta. (I DO like it.)
- Sí que lo sabe. (He DOES know it.)

**Pronoun doubling for emphasis:**
- A mí me parece bien. (To ME it seems fine.)
- A él le gusta, a mí no. (HE likes it, I don't.)

**Word order shifts:**
- Bonita es la ciudad. (Pretty is the city.) — literary emphasis

grammar_points.title: Nominalization
grammar_points.short_description: Turning verbs and adjectives into nouns
grammar_points.explanation:
**Nominalization** converts verbs and adjectives into noun-like structures.

**lo + adjective** (the... thing/part):
- lo bueno (the good thing): Lo bueno es que aprendimos.
- lo importante (the important thing): Lo importante es participar.
- lo difícil (the difficult part): Lo difícil es empezar.
- lo mejor/peor (the best/worst): Lo mejor de todo es el clima.

**el + infinitive** (the act of...):
- El vivir en la ciudad tiene ventajas. (Living in the city has advantages.)
- El saber no ocupa lugar. (Knowledge takes up no space.)

**el hecho de que + subjunctive:**
- El hecho de que trabaje mucho no significa que sea productivo.
  (The fact that he works a lot doesn't mean he's productive.)

**Suffixes for noun formation:**
- -ción (realizar → realización), -miento (conocer → conocimiento)
- -eza (bello → belleza), -dad (amable → amabilidad)

grammar_points.title: Mixed Conditionals
grammar_points.short_description: Mixing time frames in conditional sentences
grammar_points.explanation:
**Mixed conditionals** combine different time frames in the if-clause and result clause.

**Type A — Past condition → Present result:**
- Si hubiera estudiado medicina, ahora sería médico.
  (If I had studied medicine [past], I would now be a doctor [present].)
- Si no hubieras roto el jarrón, mamá no estaría enfadada.
  (If you hadn't broken the vase [past], mom wouldn't be angry [present].)

**Type B — General condition → Past result:**
- Si fuera más valiente, habría aceptado el trabajo.
  (If I were braver [general], I would have accepted the job [past].)
- Si no fuera tan tímido, le habría hablado.
  (If I weren't so shy [general], I would have spoken to her [past].)

**These are common in natural speech** and show mastery of the tense system.

grammar_points.title: Concessive Structures
grammar_points.short_description: Expressing concession: aun cuando, si bien, por más que
grammar_points.explanation:
**Concessive structures** express contrast — "even though" / "despite the fact that."

**a pesar de que** (despite the fact that):
- A pesar de que llueve, salimos. (Despite the rain, we go out.)

**si bien** (although — formal):
- Si bien es cierto que..., también... (While it's true that..., also...)
- Si bien es caro, merece la pena. (Although it's expensive, it's worth it.)

**aun cuando** (even if/when):
- Aun cuando lo supiera, no te lo diría. (Even if I knew, I wouldn't tell you.)

**por más/mucho que** (no matter how much):
- Por más que estudio, no apruebo. (No matter how much I study, I don't pass.)
- Por mucho que insistas, no cambiaré de opinión. (No matter how much you insist, I won't change my mind.)

**con + infinitive (concessive):**
- Con ser tan inteligente, no aprobó. (Despite being so intelligent, he didn't pass.)

grammar_points.title: Ellipsis in Complex Sentences
grammar_points.short_description: Omitting repeated elements for conciseness
grammar_points.explanation:
**Ellipsis** is the omission of words that can be inferred from context.

**Verb ellipsis:**
- Yo estudio español y ella, francés. (... and she [studies] French.)
- Él fue al cine y yo, al teatro. (... and I [went] to the theater.)

**Noun ellipsis (with articles):**
- ¿Qué camisa prefieres? La roja. (The red [one].)
- Quiero el grande, no el pequeño. (I want the big [one], not the small [one].)

**Comparative ellipsis:**
- Él trabaja más que yo. (He works more than I [do].)
- Este libro es mejor que aquel. (This book is better than that [one].)

**In dialogue:**
- ¿Vienes? — Sí. (Are you coming? — Yes [I'm coming].)
- ¿Lo has hecho? — Aún no. (Have you done it? — Not yet [I haven't done it].)

**Important:** Unlike English, Spanish generally does NOT omit relative pronouns:
- ✅ El libro que leí (The book that I read)
- ❌ El libro leí (The book I read)

grammar_points.title: Subjunctive Nuances
grammar_points.short_description: Fine distinctions in subjunctive use and meaning shifts
grammar_points.explanation:
At an advanced level, the **subjunctive** creates subtle meaning differences.

**Meaning shifts with mood:**
- No es que no quiero (indicative) — factual denial
- No es que no quiera (subjunctive) — softened/polite denial

**Quizá(s) / tal vez:**
- Quizás viene. (Maybe he's coming. — I think so.)
- Quizás venga. (Maybe he'll come. — I'm not sure.)

**Independent subjunctive clauses:**
- ¡Que tengas buen viaje! (Have a good trip!)
- ¡Que descansen! (Rest well!)
- ¡Ojalá llueva! (I hope it rains!)
- ¡Quién pudiera volar! (If only one could fly!)

**The "-ra" form as politeness (replacing conditional):**
- Quisiera un café. = Querría un café. (I would like a coffee.)
- Debiera usted... = Debería usted... (You should...)

**Como si + imperfect/pluperfect subjunctive:**
- Habla como si lo supiera todo. (He talks as if he knew everything.)
- Actúa como si nada hubiera pasado. (He acts as if nothing had happened.)
